region_of_origin: Singapore (Indian diaspora origin; dish roots in South Asia)

ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based mixed dish with meat/fish and spices
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for fish biryani; A0CKP (rice-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible parent category anchor. Fish biryani is a specific preparation not individually enumerated in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fish biryani as a specific food concept.

preparation_method: Dum baking (slow-cooked in a sealed pot)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Fish Biryani in Singapore is approximately 500 g, comprising a generous mound of spiced basmati rice layered with fish pieces and garnished with fried onions and fresh herbs.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; HPB Singapore Food Composition Database; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — composite estimation for fish biryani
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source contains an exact entry for Fish Biryani as prepared in Singapore. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for biryani-type rice dishes, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore food composition data for similar mixed rice dishes and USDA FoodData Central for individual components (basmati rice, fish). The input per_100g values (energy 154.55 kcal, protein 8.18 g, fat 6.36 g, carbohydrate 16.36 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN biryani composition ranges and are retained with medium confidence. Micronutrient values are cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy component data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Basmati rice, white, boiled
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fish Biryani as a composite dish. GI of 58 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white basmati rice (GI ~58, medium category), which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. Fish, spices, ghee, and yogurt components have negligible GI contribution. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 81.8 g carbohydrate in 500 g serving) / 100 = 47.4. This is a high GL per full plate, consistent with a rice-dominant mixed dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 218 mg/100 g; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 1091 mg sodium, representing 47% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Fish Biryani to a half-plate portion (250 g), avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papadums, and request minimal added salt during preparation to keep sodium intake within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 16.36 g/100 g from basmati rice; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 81.8 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 47.4, which is high and poses a meaningful postprandial glucose challenge.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to approximately 150–200 g of the total serving, increase the fish component, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to lower the overall glycemic load and blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.45 g/100 g; fish provides lean protein; dum-baked preparation avoids deep-frying and excess added fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fish Biryani is a relatively heart-friendly choice due to its low saturated fat content and lean fish protein; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request minimal ghee preparation and monitor sodium intake from the 218 mg/100 g baseline.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, cloves, coriander); low saturated fat; dum-baked preparation avoids AGE-generating deep-frying.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fish Biryani is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile; individuals sensitive to nightshades (tomatoes, chillies) should monitor their response and request reduced chilli and tomato in preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Biryani is a fragrant, layered Indian rice dish popular in Singapore's Indian-Muslim and South Indian communities. It is prepared using the traditional dum baking method, in which marinated fish (typically sea bass, dory, or snapper) and parboiled basmati rice are layered in a sealed heavy-bottomed pot and slow-cooked over low heat, allowing steam to circulate and infuse the rice with the aromatic spice marinade. The spice blend typically includes turmeric, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, chilli powder, ginger-garlic paste, and coriander, with fresh mint and fried onions added for fragrance and texture. Ghee and yogurt are used in the marinade to tenderise the fish and enrich the flavour. The resulting dish has a moist, fluffy texture with distinct layers of golden-spiced rice and tender fish pieces. Nutritionally, Fish Biryani provides approximately 155 kcal per 100 g, with 8.2 g protein, 6.4 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from fish), and 16.4 g carbohydrate from basmati rice. Sodium is moderate at 218 mg/100 g. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker and restaurant contexts. Clinically, the basmati rice base creates a medium glycemic index (~58) but a high glycemic load per full plate (~47), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The anti-inflammatory spice profile (curcumin from turmeric, gingerols from ginger) is beneficial for inflammatory conditions. Saturated fat is low at 1.45 g/100 g, making it a relatively heart-friendly choice when prepared with minimal ghee.

confidence_reason: No single approved source contains an exact entry for Fish Biryani as a composite Singapore dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN biryani-type rice dish data, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central component data. The input per_100g values are consistent with these reference ranges and are retained. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice as the dominant carbohydrate source. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Fish Biryani as a composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore, or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this composite dish in the approved sources consulted (ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central) as of 2026. trans_fat_g of 0.09 g/100 g is retained from input data; this trace level is plausible from ghee content and is consistent with USDA data for ghee-containing dishes. The three portion entries in the input (450 g, 500 g, 550 g) reflect variation across different restaurant servings; 500 g is used as the default serving size for glycemic load calculation. The input source citation (ICMR-NIN) was listed as supporting 'menu_listing' only and is not treated as a direct nutrient source; nutrient values are independently cross-validated against ICMR-NIN biryani composition data and USDA component data. FoodEx2 match type corrected to 'closest_parent' to comply with the allowed enum values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.
